rulings.Case Title: RICHARD FONG VS. PAK YAT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Action For Failure To Bring To Trial; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; And Supporting Declaration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT HAI TSE, PAK TSE's Motion To Dismiss Action For Failure To Bring To Trial. Defendants Yat and Tse's motion to dismiss is denied. Defendants are estopped from asserting the five-year deadline. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 583.140; Borglund v. Bombardier, Ltd. (1981) 121 Cal.App.3d 276, 281.) Defendants lulled plaintiff into thinking arbitration would occur, sought a continuance of the arbitration hearing set for October 26, 2018, within the five-year period, and then asserted the deadline. (See Schmidt Dec. 3; Wang Decl. 28.) The Court lacks jurisdiction to dismiss the arbitration. (See Brock v. Kaiser Found. Hosps. (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1790, 1797-1801.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH KARNAZES VS. SUMMER HOLSTROM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Hearing On Plaintiff Elizabeth Karnazes' Request For Stay
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 3, Hearing On Plaintiff Elizabeth Karnazes' Request For Stay. Plaintiff Elizabeth Karnazes' request for a stay of this action is granted until April 8, 2019. If Ms. Karnazes seeks a further stay of the action at that time, she is ordered by April 8, 2019 to serve Defendants' counsel with a declaration attaching medical records that provide evidentiary support for that request. Such declaration shall be served by hand and email on opposing counsel, and a courtesy copy must be provided to Department 302, specifying that it is for the Court's viewing and not for filing. If Defendants wish to object to the sufficiency of Ms. Karnazes's declaration and medical records, they must do so via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org by April 10, 2019 with a copy to Ms. Karnazes. If Defendants object, both parties must appear for an ex parte hearing on April 11, 2019 at 11 a.m. If Ms. Karnazes does not timely serve and lodge an adequate declaration, the Court will hold a hearing on Defendants' pending motions on April 30, 2019 at 9:30 a.m. On July 20, 2018 and January 14, 2019, the Court issued similar orders. Ms. Karnazes' submissions complied minimally with those orders. The Court admonishes Ms. Karnazes that any submission in support of a further request for a stay must attach medical records that substantiate in detail any contention that her surgeries and medical conditions will continue to preclude her from participating in this lawsuit after April 8, 2019. The Court observes that Ms. Karnazes' medical issues did not prevent her from filing in this action numerous case management statements, responses to orders to show cause, an amended complaint, a request for entry of default, statements of damages, a response to another defendant's demurrer to the first amended complaint, and several ex parte applications accompanied by lengthy declarations seeking stays of the action. In addition, the Court takes judicial notice of its own records, which establish that since this action was filed in October 2014, Ms. Karnazes has filed no fewer than nine additional lawsuits in this Court alone, eight of which were filed after her medical problems supposedly began in May 2015. The Court will take these activities into account in determining whether Ms. Karnazes has met her burden to justify any further stay of the litigation. If Ms. Karnazes seeks a further stay, her declaration shall also inform Defendants and the Court of any efforts she has made to retain counsel to represent her in this action. (See Karnazes Decl. (June 12, 2018) ["I need to retain an attorney to represent me in this matter and to file and serve an amended complaint with the help of an attorney. I do not want to represent myself in this case."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Their First Amended Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC,, MARTIN MCNERNEY DEVELOPMENT, INC.,, 418 JESSIE HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC,, 418 JESSIE PROPERTIES, LLC's Motion For Leave To File Their First Amended Cross-Complaint. Judge Schulman discloses that he is personally acquainted with Jeffrey Lowenthal, one of Defendants' counsel. This will not interfere with his ability to fairly and impartially decide this matter. Defendants Fifth Historic Properties, LLC,, Martin Mcnerney Development, Inc., 418 Jessie Historic Properties, LLC, 418 Jessie Properties, LLC's motion for leave to file a first amended cross-complaint is granted. California's liberal policy in favor of allowing amendments to pleadings, the interest in judicial economy, and plaintiff's failure to show any resulting prejudice from allowing the amendment all favor granting the motion. The viability of defendants' cross-claims against plaintiff, including the applicability and the scope of the bar under Civ. Code § 5985, may be properly tested in motions attacking the sufficiency of the pleadings. Moreover, plaintiff represents that it intends to demur to co-defendant Patrick McNerney's cross-complaint, which raises identical issues. (See Atkinson v. Elk Corp. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 739, 760 ["we believe that the better course of action would have been to allow [plaintiff] to amend the complaint and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings."]; see also Weil and Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2018) 6:644 ["Ordinarily, the judge will not consider the validity of the proposed amended pleading in deciding whether to grant leave to amend. Grounds for demurrer or motion to strike are premature. After leave to amend is granted, the opposing party will have the opportunity to attack the validity of the amended pleading."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SCOTT BRUSCELLA VS. BELAMY MILLER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 17, DEFENDANT BELAMY MILLER's MOTION TO DISMISS. Defendant Bellamy Miller's motion to dismiss the action for delay in prosecution pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 583.410 and 583.420(a)(2)(B) and California Rules of Court 3.1340 and 3.1342 is granted. The complaint in this action was filed on October 24, 2016. Plaintiff failed to exercise reasonable diligence in the prosecution of this action. He did not serve defendant with the summons and complaint for over six months, causing the Court to issue an order to show cause. The delay was not attributable to settlement negotiations or discussions. Plaintiff was not reasonably diligent in either seeking or responding to discovery. Plaintiff failed timely to respond to proper discovery requests, resulting in an order granting Defendant's motion to compel responses to form interrogatories and awarding monetary sanctions against Plaintiff and his counsel. In the more than two years after the complaint was filed and before the instant motion was filed, plaintiff did not serve any discovery. (See Landry v. Berryessa Union School Dist. (1995) 39 Cal.App.4th 691, 699 [trial court properly dismissed action, where despite "plaintiffs' claims of diligent discovery efforts, they allowed seven and one-half months to go by without activity on the case" and allowed 11 months to elapse before commencing settlement discussions].) Plaintiff also failed to exercise diligence to schedule or attend Court-ordered arbitration of the matter, causing the Court to issue multiple orders to show cause and the appointed arbitrators to lose jurisdiction. Plaintiff has not carried his burden to excuse his lack of activity or to show excusable delay. (See Lopez v. State of California (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 1292, 1295; Oskooi v. Fountain Valley Regional Hospital (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 233, 239-241 [dismissal is mandatory when there is no showing of excusable delay].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT ROBINSON ET AL VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Plaintiffs Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, February 21, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT BAHER TAMIM'S Motion To Quash Plaintiffs Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No opposition filed, motion granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CSAA INSURANCE EXCHANGE VS. ADAM BARNUM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Response To Form Interrogatory And Award Of Sanction Against Adam Barnum Dba Barnum Building & Design
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, February 21, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF CSAA INSURANCE EXCHANGE'S Motion To Compel Response To Form Interrogatory And Award Of Sanction Against Adam Barnum Dba Barnum Building & Design Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. Defendant Barnum has not established a proper basis for its objections, including that Form Interrogatory No. 17.1 violates the attorney work-product privilege. The interrogatory calls for facts not "an attorney's impressions, conclusions, opinions, or legal research or theories." Supplemental responses to be provided by March 6, 2019. Sanctions in the amount of $950 awarded to CSAA which shall be paid by March 6, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PAUL SHERWIN VS. SAN FRANCISCO STATE UNIVERSITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Board Of Trustees Of California State University Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint. (Part 2 Of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 8, Defendant Board Of Trustees Of California State University's Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint. (Part 2 Of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only). Mr. Sherwin's breach of the implied covenant cause of action was dismissed with prejudice when the court sustained the Board's demurrer to the FAC without leave to amend as to this cause of action. Furthermore, the cause of action fails because there is no viable breach of contract cause of action alleged and because "tort remedies are not available for breach of the implied covenant in an employment contract to employees who allege they have been discharged in violation of the covenant." (Foley v. Interactive Data Corp. (1988) 47 Cal.3d 654, 700.) Because plaintiff has not shown how any further amendment could cure the deficiencies of the pleading, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: PAUL SHERWIN VS. SAN FRANCISCO STATE UNIVERSITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. (part 1 of 2) Defendant the Board of Trustees of California State University's demurrer to plaintiff Paul Sherwin's second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to all causes of action. Mr. Sherwin has failed to plead facts demonstrating or excusing compliance with the claims presentation requirement of Gov't Code § 900 et seq. (State of California v. Superior Court (2004) 32 Cal.4th 1234.) Mr. Sherwin has not alleged his claim was delivered to the Office of Risk Management at the Office of the Chancellor of the California State University as required by Gov't Code § 915 (d) or that his claim was actually received by the Trustees as required under Gov't Code § 915(e)(3). Per DiCampli-Mintz v. County of Santa Clara (2012) 55 Cal.4th 983, 991, the judicial doctrine of substantial compliance does not apply to misdirected claims because the statute expressly prescribes when a misdirected claim satisfies the presentation requirement. (Id. at 992-993.) Waiver does not apply, because the Board's demurrer is not on the ground that the contents of the claim are deficient, but on the ground that the claim was not presented by the statutorily prescribed means. Estoppel does not apply because unlike in Fredrichsen v. City of Lakewood (1971) 6 Cal.3d 353, Mr. Sherwin has not alleged any facts showing that he was affirmatively misled by the Board, nor that Mr. Sherwin, who was represented by an attorney, reasonably relied on any alleged misrepresentation about where to file the claim. (See Life v. County of Los Angeles (1991) 227 Cal.App.3d 894, 902; Castaneda v. Department of Corrections & Rehabilitation (2013) 212 Cal.App.4th 1051, 1066-1067 ["[i]In general, the law 'particularly' disfavors estoppels 'where the party attempting to raise the estoppel is represented by an attorney at law.' For purposes of analyzing estoppel claims, attorneys are 'charged with knowledge of the law in California."]). Even if the SAC had sufficiently alleged satisfaction of the statutory claim presentation requirements, Mr. Sherwin's breach of contract cause of action fails as a matter of law because unlike in Shaw v. Regents of University of California (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 44, he failed to allege a specific contractual agreement that governs the relationship and is not at variance with statutorily prescribed terms and conditions. Mere appointment to a position for a specified salary is insufficient to support contract claims per Guarino v. County of Siskiyou (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 1170, 1184. Mr. Sherwin's letter of appointment expressly states that appointment is made under the "Management Personnel Plan of the California State University" and that all rights and benefits are governed by that plan. (SAC, Exh.A). Therefore the appointment was made under the authority of the management personnel plan provided for in Title 5, California State University, California Administrative Code article 2.2, sections 42720-42728. The same code governs retreat rights. (Cal. Code Regs., tit. 5, § 42723.) Furthermore Mr. Sherwin has not offered any explanation as to why he omitted previous allegations that his appointment was governed by the plan and why the court should not read them into the SAC. (RJN 1, Exh.1; RJN 2, Exh. 2); Banis Restaurant Design, Inc. v. Serrano (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 1035, 1044.)(end of part 1 of 2 of tentative ruling) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GARCIA HERNANDEZ SAWHNEY, LLP VS. STRATEGYN HOLDINGS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION And Notice Of Motion
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 9, DEFENDANT STRATEGYN HOLDINGS, LLC, STRATEGYN LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. (part 1 of 2) Defendants Strategyn Holdings, LLC and Strategyn LLC's motion for summary adjudication of the legal issue of duty is denied. With respect to written fee agreements between an attorney and a client, Business and Professions Code section 6148(a) requires an attorney, at the time the contract is entered into, to "provide a duplicate copy of the contract signed by both the attorney and the client . . . to the client." Failure to comply with any provision of section 6148 "renders the agreement voidable at the option of the client," but "the attorney shall, upon the agreement voided, be entitled to collect a reasonable fee." (Bus. & Prof. Code § 6148(c).) However, Section 6148 "shall not apply," among other grounds, if the client is a corporation. (Bus. & Prof. Code § 6148(d)(4).) The motion presents the pure legal question of whether the statutory exception to Section 6148's requirements for corporate clients applies LLCs, such that GHS had a duty to provide Defendants with a copy of the Retainer Agreement at the time it was entered into, and the Retainer Agreement is therefore voidable at Defendants' option. The only facts material to the resolution of this motion are that the Defendants are limited liability companies (LLCs) and that there was a Retainer Agreement between Plaintiff Garcia Hernandez Sawhney, LLP (GHS) and the Defendants. (SSUMF Nos. 1, 5.) Both of these facts are undisputed. The language of the Retainer Agreement does not constitute a judicial admission that GHS was bound by section 6148 because the construction of a statute and whether it applies present questions of law. (Dean W. Knight & Sons, Inc. v. State of California ex rel. Dept. of Transportation (1984) 155 Cal.App.3d 300, 305; see also Stroud v. Tunzi (2008) 160 Cal.App.4th 377, 384 ["[J]udicial admissions involve facts, not legal theories or conclusions."].) Given that the parties' briefing and the Court's research have not revealed any appellate decisions addressing this question, the Court may decide it as an issue of first impression. The Court concludes that "corporation" as that term is used in Section 6148(d)(4) includes LLCs. Statutory interpretation begins with the plain language of the statute, giving the words their usual and ordinary meaning. (People v. Castillolopez (2016) 63 Cal.4th 322, 329.) While corporations and limited liability companies are distinct legal entities, they are both of the family of corporate entities. (See Northwest Energetic Services, LLC v. California Franchise Tax Bd. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 841, 852 ["Like a corporation, an LLC is a distinct legal entity and its members (owners) have limited liability for the entity's debts and obligations."].) Significantly, the prohibition on a corporation appearing in an action in propria persona (Vann v. Shilleh (1975) 54 Cal.App.3d 192, 199) presumably applies equally to LLCs, as they are not natural persons. Similarly, even though corporations and LLCs are treated differently for purposes of taxation and corporate registration, they are treated the same in a number of respects, such as consumer protection under the Rosenthal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act and the application of the alter ego doctrine. (Civil Code 1788.1(b), 1788.2(d)-(g); Curci Investments, LLC v. Baldwin (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 214, 220-21.) Accordingly, the use of the word "corporation" does not clearly and unambiguously address the scope of the statutory exemption. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GARCIA HERNANDEZ SAWHNEY, LLP VS. STRATEGYN HOLDINGS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Strategyn Holdings, Llc, Strategyn Llc'S Motion For Summary Adjudication. (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 9, Defendant Strategyn Holdings, Llc, Strategyn Llc'S Motion For Summary Adjudication. (part 2 of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only). Courts construe statutory language to reflect the intent of the Legislature when the law was passed. (People v. Leal (2004) 33 Cal.4th 999, 1007.) Section 6148 was enacted in 1986 and California did not recognize LLCs until 1994, when the Beverly-Killea Limited Liability Company Act was passed. (Northwest Energetic, 159 Cal.App.4th at 852.) The Legislature therefore could not have deliberately excluded LLCs from the "corporation" exception of Section 6148 (d)(4). That the Legislature has not revisited this specific statutory provision and amended it to include LLCs is not dispositive given that the policy and intent of the Legislature is served by interpreting "corporation" to include a related legal entity like an LLC. The purpose of the statute is to protect vulnerable individual clients from lawyers who are not forthcoming about the costs of their legal services. (Leighton v. Forster (2017) 8 Cal.App.5th 467, 485 ["[S]ection 6148 belongs to a set of prophylactic statutes designed to assure that fee agreements are fair to the client by ensuring that the client is informed about and agrees to the terms by which the attorney who will represent him or her will be compensated."].) The Legislature implicitly expressed concern that clients who are individuals could be misled or confused by their lawyers. (See Plaintiff's Exhibit D [State Senator Presley stating that the purpose of SB 1569 was to ensure that "citizens receive fair and equitable treatment when they feel they have been wronged by an attorney"].) In contrast, the Legislature evidently concluded that corporations are sophisticated entities that did not require this type of protection. The same logic applies to LLCs, which are also sophisticated corporate entities. The intent and public policy expressed by the Legislature supports the interpretation of the "corporation" exception as including LLCs. Unlike the trial court criticized by the Court of Appeal in Benane v. International Harvester Co. (1956) 142 Cal.App.2d Supp. 874, 879, the Court is not seeking to "overrule a public policy already expressed by the Legislature" due to a change in conditions since the statute was enacted. Rather, the Court is ruling on the basis of the public policy and legislative intent embodied in the statute's legislative history and similarities between corporations and LLCs. Therefore, the Court holds that GHS did not have a statutory duty to provide Defendants with a copy of the Retainer Agreement at the time is was entered into. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BLODGETT, INC. VS. TWO A&H ENTERTAINMENT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR WRIT OF ATTACHMENT And Hearing For Right To Attach Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 11, PLAINTIFF BLODGETT, INC.'s MOTION FOR WRIT OF ATTACHMENT And Hearing For Right To Attach. Order. Plaintiff's Blodgett, Inc.'s application for a right to attach order and a writ of attachment is denied. Plaintiff has failed to carry its burden of showing the probable validity of its claim and an ascertainable amount. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 484.090(a)(2); Goldstein v Barak Construction (2008) 164 Cal.App.4th 845, 852.) Although the Janitorial Services Agreement provided that if payment were not timely received, a ten percent late fee "will be added to the following month's invoice," most of the invoices did not seek such fees, which supports defendant's assertion that plaintiff agreed to waive the late fee. (See Gamboa and Snyder Decs.) The recent invoices that seek only $3,350/month also support defendant's assertion that the parties entered into a new oral agreement. rulings.Case Title: EDUARDO PANIAGUA ET AL VS. MILESTONE FINANCIAL, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Stay Civil Action; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of William R. Stuart
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT MILESTONE FINANCIAL, LLC, BEAR BRUIN VENTURES, INC., WILLIAM STUART, CAROLYN STUART, ZOE HAMILTON's Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Stay Civil Action.(part 1 of 2) Defendants Milestone Financial, LLC, Bear Bruin Ventures, Inc., William R. Stuart, Carolyn Stuart, and Zoe Hamilton's petition to compel arbitration and stay civil action is denied. Plaintiffs allege that they were fraudulently induced to enter into the original loan agreement and later loan modification agreements by Defendants' fraudulent misrepresentations and omissions, including their alleged failures to disclose Milestone's unlicensed status and the existence of cease and desist orders against Defendants by the California Bureau of Real Estate. Plaintiffs also seek rescission of those agreements. An arbitration clause generally will not be enforced if it is found within an agreement that may properly be judicially determined to be void for fraud in the execution or fraud in the inception, such that there was never any existing agreement in the first place. (Duffens v. Valenti (2008) 161 Cal.App.4th 434, 448.) Here, as in Duffens, Plaintiffs "essentially plead that fraud in the inducement occurred, because they knew what they were signing, i.e., their agreements. However, they claim their consent was induced by fraud, so that even though there was mutual assent and a contract, the contract, by reason of the fraud, is voidable." (161 Cal.App.4th at 450.) Under California's Real Estate Law, "[i]t is unlawful for any person to engage in the business, act in the capacity of, advertise or assume to act as a real estate broker or a real estate salesman within this state without first obtaining a real estate license . . . ." (Bus. & Prof. Code § 10130.) In addition, "[n]o individual may engage in business as a mortgage loan originator" without first obtaining and maintaining a real estate license and a license endorsement identifying that individual as a licensed mortgage loan originator. (Id. § 10166.02(b).) "The purpose of the licensing requirement is to protect the public from the perils incident to dealing with incompetent or untrustworthy real estate practitioners." (Schantz v. Ellsworth (1971) 19 Cal.App.3d 289, 292-293.) Indeed, an unlicensed person who acts as a real estate broker is subject to penal consequences. (Id. §§ 10139, 10185.) A person "engaged in the business or acting in the capacity of a real estate broker or a real estate salesman" is barred from bringing or maintaining an action "for the collection of compensation for the performance of any of the acts mentioned in this article without alleging and proving he was a duly licensed real estate broker . . . at the time the alleged cause of action arose." (Id. § 10136.) The cease and desist orders attached to the petition to compel arbitration and the findings made by the Bureau of Real Estate in those orders provide substantial evidence that at or around the time Plaintiffs entered into the original residential mortgage loan agreement, Defendants Milestone and Hamilton were misrepresenting to the public that they were licensed to act as a real estate broker, real estate salesperson, and/or mortgage loan broker, when in fact they lacked the real estate broker licenses, real estate licenses, and mortgage loan originator endorsements required by statute. This evidence is sufficient to support "a preliminary factual determination that misleading circumstances existed and led to the entry into the agreements, supporting a conclusion the agreements are void. These conclusions apply to avoid the arbitration clauses contained within the agreements." (Duffens, 161 Cal.App.4th at 456.) (end of part 1 of 2 )=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HELENA PAPPAS VS. CAROLYN CHANG MD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Enforce Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 18, PLAINTIFF HELENA PAPPAS' Motion To Enforce Settlement. Plaintiff Helena Pappas' motion to enforce settlement pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section § 664.6 is denied. Ms. Pappas has failed to show that the statutory requirements for the court's exercise of jurisdiction to enter judgment pursuant to Code Civ. Proc. § 666.4 are satisfied. There was no "pending litigation" between the parties when they stipulated that their settlement may be enforced under § 664.6. (Kirby v. Southern California Edison Co. (2000) 78 Cal.App.4th 840, 844-845 [release executed before suit filed could not be enforced under § 664.6]; see also Viejo Bancorp, Inc. v. Wood (1989) 217 Cal.App.3d 200, 206 ["By its very terms, section 664.6 is limited to settlements reached in pending litigation."].) Neither party has cited any authority as to whether the term "pending litigation" in section 664.6 encompasses arbitration proceedings. However, the language of section 666.4 shows that litigation means "court proceedings" because the section enables the "court" to "retain jurisdiction" upon the request of the parties. Because there was no pending litigation between the parties when they entered into the settlement agreement, "the superior court never obtained subject matter jurisdiction" in the first instance. (Housing Group v. United Nat. Ins. Co. (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 1106.) Because the court lacks jurisdiction to enforce the settlement agreement under the summary procedure of Code of Civil Procedure § 664.6, the court does not reach the merits of Ms. Pappas' claim. (See Harris v. Rudin, Richman & Appel (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 299, 306 ["The statutory procedure for enforcing settlement agreements under section 664.6 is not exclusive. Thus, even when the summary procedures of section 664.6 are not available, a party can still seek to enforce a settlement agreement by, among other things, prosecuting an action for breach of contract."]) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Chang is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HELENA PAPPAS VS. CAROLYN CHANG MD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition To Compel Return To Arbitration And To Stay Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 18, DEFENDANT CAROLYN CHANG M.D.'s Petition To Compel Return To Arbitration And To Stay Proceedings. Defendant Carolyn Chang, M.D.'s petition to compel return to arbitration and to stay proceedings is denied. There is no agreement between the parties to arbitrate plaintiff Helena Pappas' claim for breach of contract stated in her complaint. The claim is based on breach of the settlement agreement signed as a result of the parties' voluntary mediation in the course of arbitration of Ms. Pappas' medical malpractice claim against Ms. Chang. The settlement agreement does not contain any arbitration provision and its language evidences the parties' intent to resort to court for its enforcement. Because Ms. Pappa's breach of contract claim does not constitute a "dispute as to medical malpractice," defined as "a claim arising out of or related to all treatment or services provided by the physician," it does not fall within the scope of the Patient-Physician Arbitration Agreement. (See United Teachers of Los Angeles v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2012) 54 Cal.4th 504, 516 ["It is well established that a court will not grant a petition to compel arbitration filed pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1281.2 if the subject matter to be arbitrated is not within the scope of the arbitration agreement"]). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Ms. Pappas is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TONI LESSLER VS. THE REALREAL, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Realreal, Inc.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 21, 2019, 2018, Line 15, Defendant The Realreal, Inc.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings. Defendant The RealReal Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration and staying this action is granted. Defendant's motion for an order awarding attorneys' fees and costs as sanctions is denied. Plaintiff Toni Lessler expressly consented to the Mutual Arbitration Agreement ("MAA" or "the agreement") by signing it. Defendant's signature on the MAA was not a requirement for Defendant to enforce the agreement to compel arbitration. (See Serafin v. Balco Properties Ltd., LLC (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 165, 176 ["[T]he writing memorializing an arbitration agreement need not be signed by both parties in order to be upheld as a binding arbitration agreement. . . . [I]t is not the presence or absence of a signature [on an agreement] which is dispositive; it is the presence or absence of evidence of an agreement to arbitrate which matters."]; Fagelbaum & Heller LLP v. Smylie (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 1351, 1367 [plaintiff "did not meet his burden to prove 'that the signatures of all parties were contemplated as being a condition precedent to the validity of the contract.' [Citation.] His signature individually bound him to the agreement and to the arbitration clause within it."]; Vargas v. Delivery Outsourcing LLC (N.D. Cal. Mar. 14, 2016) 2016 WL 946112 at *5 [arbitration agreement was binding on employee although it was not signed by defendant employer].) The existence of an empty employer signature block and the language stating that modifications to the MAA must be evidenced by a mutually signed writing do not render the MAA unenforceable. The MAA contains explicit language predicating enforceability on Plaintiff's signature, but no reciprocal language refers to a need for Defendant's signature. Plaintiff's reliance on Huckaba v. Ref-Chem (5th Cir. 2018) 892 F.3d 686 is misplaced as that case applied Texas law, and in any event is factually distinguishable. The court in that case recognized that "whether a signature is required to bind the parties is a question of the parties' intent," and that signatures are not required "[a]s long as the parties give their consent to the terms of the contract, and there is no evidence of an intent to require both signatures as a condition precedent to it becoming effective as a contract." (Id. at 689.) The arbitration agreement in that case involved explicit language conditioning arbitration on the signatures of both parties; such language is not present here. (See id. ["The arbitration agreement at issue here contains: (1) a statement that '[b]y signing this agreement the parties are giving up any right they may have to sue each other. . . .'"] [emphasis added].) No sanctions are awarded under Code of Civil Procedure section 128.5 because Plaintiff did not act in bad faith. Specifically, Defendant has not shown that Plaintiff opposed this motion solely to cause delay and the arguments made, while contrary to the weight of California and other precedent, were not "totally and completely without merit." (Code Civ. Proc. §128.5(b)(2).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)
